**Christopher Miles Boardman**, [CBE](/wiki/Commander_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire "Commander of the Order of the British Empire") (born 26 August 1968) is an English former racing cyclist. A [time trial](/wiki/Time_trial "Time trial") and [prologue](/wiki/Individual_time_trial "Individual time trial") specialist, Boardman won the [inaugural men's World time trial championship in 1994](/wiki/1994_UCI_Road_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_time_trial "1994 UCI Road World Championships – Men's time trial"), won the [individual pursuit](/wiki/Individual_pursuit "Individual pursuit") [gold medal](/wiki/Gold_medal "Gold medal") at the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), broke the world [hour record](/wiki/Hour_record "Hour record") three times, and won three prologue stages (and consequently wore the [yellow jersey](/wiki/Yellow_jersey "Yellow jersey") on three occasions) at the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France").

Boardman used the [Lotus 108](/wiki/Lotus_108 "Lotus 108") [time trial bicycle](/wiki/Time_trial_bicycle "Time trial bicycle") designed by [Mike Burrows](/wiki/Mike_Burrows_(bicycle_designer) "Mike Burrows (bicycle designer)") and built by the sports car manufacturer [Lotus](/wiki/Lotus_Cars "Lotus Cars"). Later he worked with the UK carbon fibre bike specialist Hotta, to produce other time-trial frame designs, which he raced in various events including world championships, and Olympic games. He is now involved in producing commercial and competition bikes with the [Boardman Bikes](/wiki/Boardman_Bikes "Boardman Bikes") and Boardman Elite ventures.

Boardman has also worked to promote walking and cycling across the UK, becoming [Greater Manchester's](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Combined_Authority "Greater Manchester Combined Authority") walking and cycling commissioner in 2017, Greater Manchester's Transport Commissioner in 2021 and most recently, Commissioner of [Active Travel England](/wiki/Active_Travel_England "Active Travel England").

In 1992, he was awarded an MBE for services to cycling. Boardman was appointed Commander of the [Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") (CBE) in [2024 Birthday Honours](/wiki/2024_Birthday_Honours "2024 Birthday Honours") for services to active travel.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life and amateur career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and amateur career")]

Boardman was educated at [Hilbre High School](/wiki/Hilbre_High_School "Hilbre High School") in [Wirral](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Wirral "Metropolitan Borough of Wirral"), Merseyside, and rode in his first bike race at the age of 13. He was on the national cycling team by the age of 16.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Boardman won his first national time trial title in the 1984 "[George Herbert Stancer](/wiki/George_Herbert_Stancer "George Herbert Stancer")" schoolboy 10-mile championship and subsequently won the 1986 junior 25-mile championship. He also broke the junior 25-mile national record in 1984.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a senior he won four consecutive [hill climb](/wiki/Hill_climbing_(cycling) "Hill climbing (cycling)") championships (from 1988 to 1991), five consecutive 25-mile championships (from 1989 to 1993), the 50-mile championship in 1991 and 1992, and the men's British time trial championship in 2000. He broke the record for 25 miles in 1992 and 1993 with 45 minutes 57 seconds (which he held until 2009)[[5]](#cite_note-5) on a course based on the A34 near Oxford. He was also a member of the winning North Wirral Velo team in the 1993 100 km [team time trial](/wiki/Team_time_trial "Team time trial") championship (in a record time of 2:00:07), having previously won the event three times with [Manchester Wheelers' Club](/wiki/Manchester_Wheelers%27_Club "Manchester Wheelers' Club"), in 1988, 1989 and 1991.

[![Lotus Type 108 bicycle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Lotus_108_%2824281585325%29.jpg/250px-Lotus_108_%2824281585325%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lotus_108_(24281585325).jpg)

The [Lotus 108](/wiki/Lotus_108 "Lotus 108") bicycle Boardman used to win the [4 km individual pursuit](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_individual_pursuit "Cycling at the 1992 Summer Olympics – Men's individual pursuit") at the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics")

At the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics") in Barcelona, Boardman rode a [Lotus 108](/wiki/Lotus_108 "Lotus 108") in the [4 km individual pursuit](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_1992_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_individual_pursuit "Cycling at the 1992 Summer Olympics – Men's individual pursuit"). [Lotus Engineering](/wiki/Lotus_Cars "Lotus Cars")'s 'uni-axle' design incorporated several new features. Boardman caught Germany's [Jens Lehmann](/wiki/Jens_Lehmann_(cyclist) "Jens Lehmann (cyclist)"), the 1991 World Champion, in the Olympic final on his way to winning the gold medal.

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Professional career")]

Having started his cycling career as a time trial specialist, Boardman turned professional with the GAN team, later renamed the [Crédit Agricole](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9dit_Agricole_(cycling_team) "Crédit Agricole (cycling team)") team, of manager [Roger Legeay](/wiki/Roger_Legeay "Roger Legeay"). His first race as a professional was the 1993 [Grand Prix Eddy Merckx](/wiki/Grand_Prix_Eddy_Merckx "Grand Prix Eddy Merckx"), a 66 km time trial which he won. He further won several stages of the Midi Libre and [Critérium du Dauphiné Libéré](/wiki/Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9_Lib%C3%A9r%C3%A9 "Critérium du Dauphiné Libéré") stage races, including the final road stage. In 1993 Boardman established the fastest time for a bicycle around the 37.73 mile [Snaefell Mountain Course](/wiki/Snaefell_Mountain_Course "Snaefell Mountain Course"), the course used for the [Isle of Man TT Races](/wiki/Isle_of_Man_TT_Races "Isle of Man TT Races"). Riding a specially modified bicycle, Boardman recorded a time of 1hr 23min 54secs. The time recorded would prove to be the longest standing cycling record on the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), until it was beaten by [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh") in 2015. Kennaugh, riding a standard racing bicycle, beat the record by six seconds.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Boardman competed with [Graeme Obree](/wiki/Graeme_Obree "Graeme Obree") for the [hour record](/wiki/Hour_record "Hour record") using radically modified time-trial bikes, beating each other's records in turn; in one eight-month period in 1994 the record fell four times.[[7]](#cite_note-Grdn-7) Boardman won the prologue of the [1994 Tour de France](/wiki/1994_Tour_de_France "1994 Tour de France") with what was then the fastest time ever recorded.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In the process he caught [Luc Leblanc](/wiki/Luc_Leblanc "Luc Leblanc"), who had previously been dismissive about Boardman's 1993 hour record, claiming that it could probably be beaten by half the professional peloton.[[9]](#cite_note-9) However, he subsequently lost the [yellow jersey](/wiki/Yellow_jersey "Yellow jersey") in a [team time trial](/wiki/Team_time_trial "Team time trial"). He was hailed as the UK's future Tour de France winner, despite his own insistence that it was a long shot. After retirement he said he was not able to recover from the rigours of stage racing due to a low hormone profile. "I've always had it, it's probably been that way since I was born, but because of the type of racing that I did in the past, it was not a problem."

In the [1995 Tour de France](/wiki/1995_Tour_de_France "1995 Tour de France"), Boardman crashed in the prologue and withdrew due to his injuries. The [1996 Tour de France](/wiki/1996_Tour_de_France "1996 Tour de France") saw him return in the wet and rainy prologue where he was beaten by [Alex Zülle](/wiki/Alex_Z%C3%BClle "Alex Zülle") and finished in second place. Boardman won bronze in the 52 km road time trial at the [1996 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics") in Atlanta. He did not defend his track individual pursuit title.

Boardman made a comeback at the [1997 Tour de France](/wiki/1997_Tour_de_France "1997 Tour de France"), winning the prologue of the Tour once more this time on a on a [Hotta TT](/w/index.php?title=Hotta_TT&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hotta TT (page does not exist)"), although a crash forced him to quit the tour on stage 13.

In [1998 Tour de France](/wiki/1998_Tour_de_France "1998 Tour de France"), when the Tour began in Dublin, Ireland, Boardman won the prologue, but this time crashed out of the race on stage 2.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1998 he was diagnosed with [osteopenia](/wiki/Osteopenia "Osteopenia") due to low testosterone levels.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Treatment to prevent a worsening of his condition would have required him to take [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone_(medication) "Testosterone (medication)"), which is banned under anti-doping rules. The UCI refused to allow Boardman an exemption on medical grounds. Faced with either retiring to allow treatment for his osteoporosis, or continuing to cycle without taking testosterone, Boardman chose to continue in cycling untreated for a further two years, hoping to finish his career on a high note at the [2000 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1997 the [Union Cycliste Internationale](/wiki/Union_Cycliste_Internationale "Union Cycliste Internationale") (UCI) had changed their regulations for the hour record, restricting competitors to roughly the same equipment that [Eddy Merckx](/wiki/Eddy_Merckx "Eddy Merckx") had used in the 1970s, banning [time trial](/wiki/Time_trial "Time trial") helmets, disc or tri-spoke wheels, aerodynamic bars and [monocoque](/wiki/Monocoque "Monocoque") composite frames. Boardman made an attempt at the hour record using this new ruling in 2000 and rode 49.441 km, just 13 m further than Merckx's 28-year-old record.[[7]](#cite_note-Grdn-7)

Boardman's preparation for the [road time trial at the 2000 Olympics](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_2000_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_road_time_trial "Cycling at the 2000 Summer Olympics – Men's road time trial") was affected by missing the [2000 Tour de France](/wiki/2000_Tour_de_France "2000 Tour de France") due to sinus problems;[[13]](#cite_note-13) he finished eleventh in the time trial.[[2]](#cite_note-sports-reference-2)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Boardman retired after the Olympics, at the age of 32.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In an interview Boardman said that the last two years of his professional career were the most difficult, especially because of his debilitating health condition and also marital issues. Boardman's [osteoporosis](/wiki/Osteoporosis "Osteoporosis") was uncommon for someone as young as he was. He was criticised for not realising his potential, but in response he said that, "I never considered myself particularly gifted, but I managed to stretch and mould the ability that I have, and found a niche for myself."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Work after retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Work after retirement")]

Since retirement from professional cycling, Boardman has undertaken a range of roles including television punditry, advising the British cycling teams and working in walking and cycling advocacy roles.

Boardman was appointed a technical adviser to the British road and track cycling team in 2004, and was equipment and technical manager to the TeamGB cyclists at the 2008 Beijing Olympic Games.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Boardman has a brand of cycles and accessories under the name [Boardman Bikes](/wiki/Boardman_Bikes "Boardman Bikes").[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)[[21]](#cite_note-:1-21) He is also involved in producing competition cycles through Boardman Elite.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-22)

### Walking and cycling advocacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Walking and cycling advocacy")]

Boardman has worked in various walking and cycling advocacy roles. He first took up these roles after his young daughter asked to ride to the park with him, in the northern seaside town where they lived; Boardman refused, thinking it too dangerous. He said it felt very wrong that he, an ex-Olympic cyclist, did not feel he could keep his child safe on a one-minute 550m ride, so he decided to do something about it.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

He is an [advocate of policies to greatly increase utility cycling](/wiki/Cycling_advocacy "Cycling advocacy") in the United Kingdom, citing the potential to reduce the 35,000 annual deaths from obesity-related diseases, and urging that in road traffic accidents there be a presumption of guilt on the driver of the larger vehicle.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In July 2016, his mother Carol was killed by a motor vehicle while cycling;[[25]](#cite_note-25) the driver was jailed for causing death by dangerous driving.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Boardman was appointed Greater Manchester's first Cycling and Walking Commissioner by [Andy Burnham](/wiki/Andy_Burnham "Andy Burnham") in July 2017.[[28]](#cite_note-28) He developed a plan to create 1,800 miles of protected walking and cycling routes.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In May 2021, he became Greater Manchester’s first Transport Commissioner.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Boardman was appointed as Interim Commissioner for the government's new cycling and walking body, [Active Travel England](/wiki/Active_Travel_England "Active Travel England"), in January 2022,[[31]](#cite_note-31) and permanently as Commissioner from June 2022, leaving his role at Greater Manchester.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Television pundit and commentator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television pundit and commentator")]

Boardman has worked as a contributor to cycling programmes on both [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") and [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)"), including commentary at the [Olympics Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") and at the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France"). For the BBC, he has worked on five [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") since [Beijing 2008](/wiki/Beijing_2008 "Beijing 2008") and four [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") since [Delhi 2010](/wiki/Delhi_2010 "Delhi 2010") as a cycling commentator.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chris_Boardman&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Boardman lives with his wife and six children in his native [Wirral](/wiki/Wirral_Peninsula "Wirral Peninsula"). In 1992, he was awarded an MBE for services to cycling.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 2009 Boardman took part in the London marathon, finishing in 3hrs 19min 27sec. He was also inducted into the [British Cycling Hall of Fame](/wiki/British_Cycling_Hall_of_Fame "British Cycling Hall of Fame").[[42]](#cite_note-BChalloffame-42) In 2024, he was further awarded a CBE for services to active travel.[[43]](#cite_note-43)
